agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting, not a formal specification. It lacks precise thresholds, boundary definitions, and operational details.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing / Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to some baseline or capacity — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes" },
      { "term": "Real-time adjustment", "definition": "Price changes that occur dynamically in response to current demand conditions, not batch or scheduled" },
      { "term": "Base price", "definition": "Implied but unstated — the reference price from which dynamic adjustments are made" },
      { "term": "Delivery price", "definition": "The price charged to the customer for a delivery, subject to dynamic adjustment" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjusted in real time based on current demand levels",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy about pricing strategy. A VP could decide to switch to batch pricing or fixed pricing. It is not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand conditions change, the system recalculates delivery prices without manual intervention and within a defined latency window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand is high (relative to baseline/capacity), prices must increase above the base price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy — surge pricing. Leadership could change the demand-price relationship at any time.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price is greater than the base price for the same route/service"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand is low (relative to baseline/capacity), prices must decrease below the base price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a competitive pricing policy. Leadership could decide to hold base price during low demand instead of discounting.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand is below the low-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price is less than the base price for the same route/service"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual approval step in the pricing loop",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy. The business could add manual overrides or approval gates at any time.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a demand change event, when the system recalculates prices, then no human approval is required and the new price is applied automatically"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Prices must be bounded by guardrails — a maximum ceiling and minimum floor — to prevent extreme pricing",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without price bounds, the system could produce zero or negative prices (destroying revenue) or infinite prices (destroying trust and potentially violating consumer protection law). This is a state integrity constraint: a price outside sane bounds represents a corrupt system state.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every calculated delivery price P: P >= configured_price_floor AND P <= configured_price_ceiling"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary amount greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical — it means paying the customer to deliver. This is a fundamental data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every calculated delivery price P: P > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The price presented to a customer at booking time must be the price they are charged (price lock on acceptance)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a fundamental commerce invariant. If the price changes between quote and charge, the system is in an inconsistent state and likely violates consumer protection regulations. The CEO didn't state this but it is an implied physical constraint of any pricing system.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a customer accepts a delivery at quoted price Q, when the delivery is completed, then the charged amount equals Q regardless of subsequent demand changes"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The demand signal used for pricing must reflect current conditions, not stale data",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The staleness threshold is a tunable operational parameter, not a hard constraint. But the intent of 'real time' implies recency matters.",
      "testable_assertion": "The demand data used in any price calculation must have been sampled within a configured maximum-age window"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is unavailable or delayed (system outage, data lag)", "expected_behavior": "System must fall back to a safe default price (likely base price) rather than failing to quote or using infinitely stale data. Guardrails must still apply." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (e.g., event ends, sudden weather change)", "expected_behavior": "Price should not whipsaw — some smoothing or dampening mechanism is likely needed to avoid customer confusion and arbitrage. NOT stated in spec." },
    { "scenario": "Demand is exactly at the boundary between 'high' and 'low'", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. What is 'normal' demand? The spec implies a binary (high/low) but reality requires at least three bands or a continuous function." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple concurrent price calculations for same route at demand boundary", "expected_behavior": "All customers requesting quotes at the same moment for the same route should see the same price (consistency). Race conditions could cause different prices." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail values are misconfigured (floor > ceiling, or floor = 0)", "expected_behavior": "System should reject invalid guardrail configurations at deploy/config time, not at price calculation time." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price caps in certain jurisdictions (e.g., emergency/disaster surge pricing laws)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails may need to be jurisdiction-aware. Not addressed in spec but critical for legal compliance." },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price, refreshes, sees a different price seconds later", "expected_behavior": "This is technically correct per the spec ('real-time') but may destroy customer trust. UX implications not addressed." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Revenue optimization through demand-responsive pricing. Risk of revenue loss if guardrails are too tight (underpricing during peak) or customer churn if too loose (gouging). Discounting during low demand could erode margins if base costs aren't covered.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand measurement infrastructure, price calculation engine, and monitoring. Fully automated system means operational risk shifts from manual pricing errors to algorithmic pricing errors at scale.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing — some will benefit (low demand discounts), others will feel penalized (surge pricing). Transparency of pricing rationale is critical for trust. Comparison shopping behavior will increase.",
    "risk": "High risk of regulatory exposure (surge pricing during emergencies), reputational damage (perceived gouging), and algorithmic errors at scale. 'Fully automated' with vague 'guardrails' is under-specified for a system that directly affects revenue and customer trust.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Variable delivery prices; winners during low demand, losers during high demand. Trust depends on transparency and fairness perception." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue becomes less predictable, harder to forecast. Need new reporting on dynamic pricing performance." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Demand measurement feeds into pricing — ops data quality directly affects revenue. New coupling between operations and pricing." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Increased complaint volume about price variability. Need scripts and policies for price dispute resolution." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review surge pricing against consumer protection and price gouging regulations per jurisdiction." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "If courier compensation is tied to delivery price, their earnings become variable. If not, margin impact falls entirely on the company." },
      { "group": "Sales/Partnerships", "impact": "Enterprise/contract customers may demand fixed pricing, creating a two-tier pricing complexity." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a casual CEO statement at an all-hands, not a formal requirements document. It uses vague language ('probably', 'some guardrails', 'don't go crazy'), provides no thresholds, no definition of demand, no pricing formula, no guardrail values, no latency requirements, and no regulatory considerations. Almost every operational detail must be assumed or invented. This is a vision, not a specification.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices adjust based on demand (core concept is clear)",
      "High demand = higher prices, low demand = lower prices (directional relationship is explicit)",
      "System is automated (stated directly)",
      "Guardrails exist to bound prices (stated, though vaguely)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition of 'demand' — volume? capacity ratio? geographic? temporal?",
      "Definition of 'real time' — sub-second? minutes? per-order?",
      "Guardrail thresholds — what are the min/max bounds?",
      "Pricing function shape — linear? stepped? exponential?",
      "What constitutes the 'base price' from which adjustments are made",
      "Whether low-demand discounting goes below cost",
      "Price lock semantics — when is a quoted price committed?",
      "Regulatory and jurisdictional constraints",
      "Whether couriers/drivers are affected by price changes",
      "Whether enterprise/contract customers are exempt"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A 'base price' exists from which adjustments are made (not stated but logically required)",
      "Prices must be positive (physical/financial constraint, not stated)",
      "Price quoted at booking is the price charged (standard commerce invariant, not stated)",
      "'Guardrails' means both a ceiling and a floor (could mean only a ceiling)",
      "Demand is measured per geographic area or route (not stated)",
      "'Competitive' implies awareness of market rates (no mechanism described)",
      "'Profitable' implies prices must cover costs (no cost model described)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The term 'demand' is undefined. It could mean: (A) absolute order volume, (B) order volume relative to courier capacity, (C) order volume relative to historical baseline, or (D) a composite signal. The entire pricing model depends on this definition.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Operations", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Define demand as capacity utilization ratio", "Define demand as volume vs. historical baseline", "Define demand as a composite score"],
      "recommendation": "Define demand as capacity utilization ratio (orders / available couriers) as it most directly reflects the supply-demand economics the CEO described."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Probably have some guardrails' is not a commitment. Are guardrails required for launch? What are the bounds? Is there a price floor, ceiling, or both? What values?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Make guardrails mandatory with configurable bounds", "Make guardrails optional and launch without them", "Launch with conservative guardrails and tune over time"],
      "recommendation": "Guardrails must be mandatory (not optional) with both floor and ceiling. Launch with conservative bounds. This is a risk management necessity, not a nice-to-have."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Real time' is undefined. Could mean per-request recalculation, sub-second updates, minute-level batches, or hourly adjustments. Latency requirements drive architecture.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Per-request calculation", "Sub-minute recalculation with caching", "Minute-level batch recalculation"],
      "recommendation": "Per-request calculation with cached demand signals updated at sub-minute intervals. Balances 'real time' intent with engineering feasibility."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory or legal review has been mentioned. Dynamic/surge pricing is regulated in many jurisdictions, especially during emergencies or disasters. Some regions ban surge pricing entirely.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Proceed without legal review", "Block launch until legal review completes", "Launch in unregulated jurisdictions first"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review must complete before any launch. Jurisdiction-specific guardrail overrides should be a first-class feature, not an afterthought."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The relationship between 'competitive' and 'profitable' is potentially contradictory. Being competitive may require pricing below profitability during low demand. No cost floor or margin requirement is stated.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Price floor = cost (never sell below cost)", "Price floor = cost + minimum margin", "Allow below-cost pricing as a growth strategy with separate approval"],
      "recommendation": "Price floor must be at or above variable cost per delivery. Selling below cost should require explicit policy override, not be a default algorithmic outcome."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
